speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number two.

speaker: interviewer
text: Martin, could you continue with your story?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and I was thinking, what am I-- I listened to this, you know, to this-- to the radio program. It's-- the heater's working full blast-- nice and warm, and-- nice and comfortable. And I thought to myself, gee, just like my father. He had to walk the 14 kilometers home. He'd come home like an ice man, all frozen up. And here, I'm sitting in my car-- nice and warm, listening to a program from Boston, Massachusetts.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was thinking to myself, what am I gonna have for-- for dinner or for a snack when I get home? Because I was getting home about 10 o'clock already. And I could not decide what I'm gonna have. And I thought to myself, gee, we have two refrigerators-- one of the kitchen, one in the basement. And they're fully loaded.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was always lots of food in my refrigerator-- in our refrigerator. I don't know whether I made sure or whether my wife made sure, but there was always-- you name it. Ice cream in the winter, cheeses, milk, butter, fruits, vegetables, bread-- there's no question about that-- cookies, eggs. But I could not decide. I'm sure there were frozen chickens, you know, raw ones and cooked ones. I could not decide what to have for dinner. And I thought to myself, it wasn't like that over there. It was rough. The people in general in our village were very nice to my father. There were-- I think they had some kind of a respect for him-- or whether he was adapted to them well. Whatever it is, was very-- they were very nice. I think they were nicer to my father than to my mother, and my mother was a native of that village. She was born there. She was raised there. And she lived there all her life.

speaker: interviewer
text: My father participated in the activities in the community. There was no television, no radio. What do you do? You go to your neighbor's, to some neighbor's. You know, the men got together, and probably the women can visit too. And they had probably a couple of vodkas. They used to sit and talk and joke around.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but he was always invited. He was always going somewhere. Whereas my mother was a homebody. She-- she used to get very upset that he used to go out over there to-- to be buddy buddies with his friend, with people of the village.

speaker: interviewer
text: If there wasn't-- we had a fire department-- a little fire department. Well, it was run by volunteers. There was a fire. We lived, like, maybe half a block from the fire station. And there were times when my father had a horse. Guess what? He hitched up the horse. He ran to the fire station, hitched the horse up to a barrel of water, because the water was always ready. And he used to run to help out to put out the fire.

speaker: interviewee
text: But usually the fire is not close to the fire station. It's far away. By the time you got there with the water, the place was burned down anyway. But the thought, there, you know, Kobylanski came to help out with the fire. How nice! After awhile, they gave up. You know, they didn't praise him. It was taken for a given. If there were some parties, he was always invited. Maybe he wasn't. I don't know. But he was there. One particular time I remember, my mother sent me to fetch Father home because he's gonna become an alcoholic. He drinks too much. So I went there. And she knew where it was, so she sent me over there. And I came, actually opened-- you didn't knock on the door. You opened up the door, and you went in. And I go in there, look around the house-- no Father. Most of the cottages in our village, the people-- the natives, the Ukranians-- they had like two rooms-- a living room which was the kitchen, the living room, the dining room, and the family room. That was a-- the benches around the wall. The-- the big oven was in one corner. Icons all the way around the room-- you know, on the wall, hanging icons. They were propped up on shelves most of the time-- all these holy people, standing. We didn't have any-- no images of God or Jesus or anything. As Jews do not have images because, thou shall not have graven images.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I looked for my father. He's not there. So I try to figure out what happened. So they ask me, what do you want? I said, I came to pick up my father. My mother said the my father has to come home. She said, sorry, your father's not here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he's not here, so I'm ready to turn around and go home. They said, wait a minute. I think we found your father. He was hiding behind the bench. [LAUGHS] They hid him behind the bench. They said to me, and tell your mother, your father did not eat pork here. He did not eat any unkosher foods. He had bread and garlic for a chaser.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was-- you know, I don't know if I-- I don't remember how I felt about it, but it was-- it stuck in my memory. You know. Tell your mother that your father did not eat unkosher foods. He had bread and garlic, you know, for a chaser-- to make sure that she understood. What were the religious holidays like before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: For Jews?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: They were very nice. We went to-- to the synagogue. One of the houses, Mr. Yechiel-- Mr. Yechiel Halperin had a house of his own. And that's where the Torahs were kept, and that's where the religious services used to be conducted. And we always had a quorum. You have to have a quorum of 10 men for religious-- conduct religious services. We were never short of a quorum. All the male Jews that were of age to be counted in the quorum were always in the synagogue-- Saturdays also. I remember I used to go with my father. He used to put on his suit and his shirt. They had French-- shirts with French cuffs, with those cuff links. And there was no mud down the street. And our streets were not paved. It was just plain dirt. If it was mud, which had-- we went, gingerly not to get our shoes too dirty. He was tall. He was nice looking-- to me at that time-- and used to go to the synagogue. And on the way home, naturally, the same procedure. Sometimes we were accompanied by the other-- the other Jewish people to go home. But I think it was a-- a-- a custom-- or was it some kind of a procedural system-- that two Jews on Sabbath, on the Saturday, did not walk home together. Because I think it was understood that that's a no-no for Jews to congregate in the street on a Sabbath. So that means there were some kind of undercurrents in the community that did not tolerate that. Because Mr. Adler and his sons--

speaker: interviewer
text: You know, he had three sons who were old-- the sons were older than me so then they're part of the quorum, right? And so they used to go in a different way home. Mr. Osher and his son went a different way home too. My uncles, maybe they went. They could never walk with us. But you-- when I got home, I used to see them walking home. So that's probably what was the-- the-- the reason for it-- that there was a feeling that Jews did not walk on Saturday together from the synagogue. Because that's a holiday, right? You show off. So either it's a-- a sign of solidarity or a sign of religious observance. So the rest of the community, I think, did not tolerate it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But my father had a few friends-- not a few, many friends. And some were very significant, because they were-- they instructed us when times were bad how to survive. He used to talk to this one particular man, a Mr. Czepko-- C-Z-E-P-K-O. It's later that I got to know that he was a, let's say, an intellectual. In that community, a man that read a book or a newspaper or sent his children to-- to a university or college was an intellectual. I'm not talking about Jewish people. I'm talking about the Christians, the Ukrainians. And it's from them that my father learned the art of survival.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the holidays were very nice. There used to be problems with Christian holidays. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Easter time was bad, because on the eve of Easter, they either smashed your doors or they smashed your windows or they took away your horse and buggy. They didn't steal it. They used to take the wagon and put it up someplace way up on the highest hill you could get. The only way you could bring it down, you had to take the whole wagon apart-- you know, to take the wheel. You had to walk each piece part of the wagon down. That's a lot of work. You know, that's a day's work.

speaker: interviewee
text: But, you know, the Easter Resurrection and-- and the death of Jesus, that was all connected with that so we've got to get even with somebody. Well, the Jews used to get it in the neck for that. It was not too violent. I mean, there was no violence. But you know, if you break a window, you have to replace it. Man, that's-- that's a big loss. You have to go to the city. You have to have the measurements. You have to bring the glass. And all-- all of that takes-- takes money. To go there and back is no problem. You have to have the money to get it. There were specially holidays when the refugees had to report for-- for the Polish army. That was back to same kind of things. Your outhouse was taken away. You know. It's a minor thing, you know. It's important because you have to replace it. The windows were to be-- again, the same thing. The horse taken away, the door smashed, the wagon hidden somewheres. Used to take two or three days before you found the wagon. You know, you have a horse. You can-- you can travel with him. There was problems, but on the whole-- now I understand those problems. At that time, you know, it was a minor inconvenience, because I didn't go look for the horse. I didn't go look for the-- for the wagon. My thing was to go have a good time-- either go to school. But the people used to talk about it. Some-- I mean-- I mean, the Christians, the Ukrainians, some thought it was funny-- that it was cute. And some-- some people thought it's not fair. That's not the right. Things like that should not be done.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did the authorities treat you in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we hardly had any authorities. There was only a mayor of the village. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- most of the problems were religious problems. You see, you could not go complain that you're being discriminated. You could not-- who you gonna go to complain to? Though the incidents were [? obvious-- ?] you know, hiding the horse-- what are you gonna-- you gonna tell the policeman that-- that-- Besides, you had to go to the county seat to report it. It takes a day. A Jew go to the county seat-- over to the county town-- to the-- to complain about smashed windows? It's only in a Jew's imagination. It did not happen. So people, Jews, did not complain. They had to take what they-- what was given. Because if you had to take a day off to go to a little thing called [INAUDIBLE]-- that was the closest official, you know, city, town, or village-- why go? You're going to be a loser anyway. When did you first become aware that the War was approaching and the Jewish people were suffering?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1941. 1941, I really became aware. I became aware that something was not right, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just a little bit before-- the summer was when the Russians were in already. But there was some-- I-- I was aware before the war that there's something is happening, but I couldn't understand what-- exactly what. And you-- when you're-- what? That has to be I was seven, eight, nine? You don't go and ask people about politics-- adults. You know, how did you get to know that, you know-- you know about politics-- like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: But there was one incident that I remember when Poland and Hungary signed a peace treaty-- or some kind of an association. And we had all the flags. Everybody had to have a flag-- a Polish and an Hungarian flag displayed. That was a problem-- no money to buy a Hungarian flag. And you have to-- there were certain specifications that the flag had to be. Terrible-- my mother screamed and hollered at my father, and what could he do? What's he going to tell the man, the government? I have no money to buy a flag? And then I remember, one of the girls down the street said to me once-- I don't know for what reason-- she says, [? Moshko-- ?] that was Ukrainian name for Moses-- you wait. We're gonna get you. We're going to get even with you. Wait till Hitler comes here. Who's he? So I went home, and I asked my parents. And, you know, they told me, don't associate with her. That she's not your friend. You should not play with her. Where do you go? That's when I became aware that something was not right.

speaker: interviewee
text: At the same time-- a little bit something during hat same time-- they begin to arrest people in the village. You know, this-- the arrests were usually done by the Polish government during the night. When you came in the morning, you heard, oh, this man was taken away. And this man, the police took away. And this one took away, and they found a spy over there in the barn. It wasn't actually a spy, he was an activist. But he had to be underground, so he was in the barn. And they arrested a lot of young people-- young men. But later on, I figured out this was in the summer. The arrests were done early in the summer-- so, like, June. But I figured out later on after the War ended. You know, you just think. You grow up. You begin to understand things. Those were the people-- excuse me-- that were attending college-- gymnasiums. That's why all the political indoctrination, political education, political activism was being conducted. But when they came home for the summer, they were easy picking. So that's why they used to be arrested. I found out later. But up till then, I didn't know, you know, there was all this stuff going on. Later, when the Soviets took over-- when the Soviets took over Poland in 1939, all of these young people were let out of the jails. Political prisoners were all liberated by the Soviets. Ah! It was nice. Everybody was happy. Our young men are back.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I want to tell you. None of the Jewish people were ever arrested for political activism, because they did not participate. They knew that you've got to keep your nose clean and stay away from politics. I think they did, because never happened. And when they came-- the Russians-- about a year later, guess what they did? They rearrested all of those that they liberated from the Polish jails. They all got arrested again-- same thing, during the night.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I remember one morning there were trucks in the village, and that was a rarity. You know, those covered trucks. They look like-- with big canvas-- like army-- like army trucks. There were two or three left of those trucks, and there were-- but the were closed. You don't know what's in there. But they'd let-- my friends-- my little friends told me there were people arrested in there, spies. They were not spies. They were natives who the Russians rearrested and shipped them out. And the reason they were-- all of this activity with the arrests of the natives were they politically active as nationalists. You know. We call them freedom fighters, fighters for independence. Over there, they're called nationalists. So that's-- that's when I became aware. I also became aware in 1939-- I was nine years old, or almost nine years old-- when the Russians took over Poland. And I remember it was sometime during the Jewish holiday of Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur-- New Year and Day of Atonement. It was a nice-- it was very nice weather at that time. But I remember because between Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur is a week, and you could run barefoot and in the streets at that time in September-- or whatever time. It had to be sometime in September. There was a nice weather. One morning, I got up, got dressed to go outside and boy, a big army in our village. Big, humongous-- and wherever you look on the road, it's full of soldiers. Well, I found out that it's the Russians. They were dressed-- soldiers-- but everybody had a red star on their cap, you know. And the-- the uniforms were different than-- than the Polish uniforms. And there were trucks, and there were tanks. And there were horses pulling cannons and stuff I'd never seen in my life before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you've got to go where the crowd is, yeah? The crowd is-- I came over there. I think I was Johnny-come-lately. And all my little friends are there and the teenagers and everybody. And I go up there. I have to see exactly what's going on. So I got up to the front, and the soldier picks me up and puts me on top of the hood of the car. Ah! We are big children, right? Most popular guy in town, sitting on top of the hood. He says to me a few words, you know, jokes. Then he says, do you know who your father is? Sure, I know who my father? Who is he? My father's Anshel. Yeah? Yeah. Where is he? I says, see my house over there? I'm trying to point out the house to him. And he laughs, and I know-- the other soldiers that are standing with him. He asked me again. Oh, definitely. My father's name is Anshel, and he's right over there. That's my father. He says, no, sonny boy. I want you to pay attention to me. From now on, if somebody asks you who your father is, you say your father's Joseph Stalin. I told him, I never-- no, it's not true. I never heard. He said, why not? I said, I never heard of that man before. He says, I'm telling you. It'll be very nice for you if they ask you who your father is, you say Joseph Stalin.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you know, up till today, there was so much laughing-- you know, not by the natives, by the soldiers, by the Russian soldiers. They thought it was the funniest thing on earth-- never forget it. That's when I became aware that something was going on. After that, they began to-- I became aware about Hitler and Germany. But I really did not-- I only heard the talks. I did not understand what they implied. I should have asked, but I don't know. I didn't know who to ask. I think I did a wise thing. I didn't ask my parents. I would have probably got a spanking for asking stupid questions.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did the threats against the Jews in your village begin?

speaker: interviewee
text: About a month. When the war started, the Germans invaded Russia-- part of Russia-- where we lived in June of what, 21? June 21. July-- in July of 1941, we were in trouble. I understood either way if there were German soldiers coming by-- because our village was close to the highway. The highway was from Lvov to Kiev-- was one-- one big stretch on like a freeway. It wasn't a freeway, because who could use a freeway? It was from [INAUDIBLE]. But the Russians built a two-lane road. They established one. But it was paved-- you know, blacktop. Before then, the road was made out of stones, you know, those round stones like, I don't know, must have been artificially made, but they were-- when you rode in a horse and wagon, wagon goes-- shook all the way. And you picked up every stone that the wheel went on. Well, the Russians built a beautiful asphalt, like, you know, blacktop highway from there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Germans, when they were going east [NON-ENGLISH] marching to the east, some of them used to come into the village, you know, look for chickens, you know, fruits and vegetables-- mostly fruits and vegetables they wanted because the army probably didn't provide them. And the kids used to send the-- the Germans to, you know, point out to me that [NON-ENGLISH], a Jew. Most of them used to say to the kids, [GERMAN]. Kill him. But they didn't understand German. But Yiddish-- what we spoke at home-- is a derivative from German, so I taught [GERMAN]. That meant 'kill him,' so the kids said, what he says? He told us to go home. Oy, he says, you know, that's not true. What did he say? I said, he told us to go home. So we gathered up and we went. My friends, we went home together. So it's already things began to cook. Then, you know, every generation replaces-- replaces itself, right? Those politically active people that the Russians did not released during the war, when Germany invaded, they were replaced by other people-- by other college students. Those were the-- I've concluded on my own that those were the-- the troublemakers. Those were-- those were the people that made life for us miserable. I'm not saying that their parents were any better, but their parents were more humane-- more understanding what life, what human beings are. Young people are only interested instant gratification. And I think they were indoctrinated, you know, by the Germans. I don't know this was done that, you know, you've got to clean out the Jews. That it's OK. You have our permission to kill it, because the German policy was anyway then, right? So they gave them their OK. If you kill a Jew, you know, you'll be OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I think it's not that-- you know, killing is not that simple when you live in a little village and you know people face-to-face. You have to have the intellectuals approve it. And the intellectual-- the intellectuals, their parents-- their father and mother, their uncles, their aunts, or maybe even their grandfathers-- and when it came to do this thing-- Because our village was ready. One day, they organized horse and buggy. They said-- you, you, you to their neighbors. You' bring your horse and wagon. Today we're going to take the Jews to the ghetto-- to Rovne. And all the men were called up during the night [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to come into a certain place-- the most popular place there, which was like a-- like a motel, a early version. It wasn't actually, but it was an inn. And they had, you know, nice facilities. They had basements and cellars and ice, and-- because you have to provide all those services. And that's when it all started. That was the beginning of all the destruction of our village. Not by strangers, by natives-- people that you knew every day, people that you saw every day except when they-- when they were away to college. And when the Germans came in and they came home, that's when things became very difficult. By that--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's stop right there. That's a  good place to stop.
